posts.caption:
Since graduating from Central Saint Martins in 2001, @henrikvibskov has become known for his expansive, often theatrical universes – collections that spider into environments, where garments live inside odd scenography, immersive soundscapes, with a distinct sense of humour.⁠
⁠
In Paris for a panel on craftsmanship at the Maison du Danemark, the Danish designer is, as ever, sliding between disciplines and ideas – clothing, installation, music and performance. His latest collection, Frog Carry Frog, began with the sighting of a fire-belly frog and expanded into a meditation on shared weight, collective labour and the unseen systems that sustain life. A typically Vibskovian starting point: unexpected, textured and bubbling with surreal possibility. If the designer’s immense creative practice transcends any tidy classification, it’s simply human nature to him. ⁠
⁠
The designer meets @mad.e.leiner in Paris to answer 20 questions: on frogs, fabrics, foot massages – and the importance of keeping things a little bit complicated. Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Courtesy of the designer⁠
1. Henrik Vibskov. Photography by Louis Poulsen⁠
2. Henrik Vibskov Autumn/Winter 2026. Photography by James Cochrane⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
As the daughter of the model and singer Karen Elson and musician and record label co-founder Jack White, @scarbarbar grew up in Nashville ensconced in a home life that was “beautifully atypical”. There was noise and there was chaos. There were demanding travel schedules. There was a house full of instruments and a studio in the garden. ⁠
⁠
“My little brother and I would wave goodbye as my dad loaded gear onto a tour bus or as my mom caught a last-minute plane to Milan. Despite that, there was a surprising sense of normalcy,” the 19-year-old tells @rose_dodd. At the link in bio, Scarlett White tells is why she has always been interested in human behaviour and how it influences making music 📲⁠
⁠
This story is taken from the Spring/Summer 2026 issue of AnOther Magazine.⁠
⁠
Photography by @mark_peckmezian⁠
⁠
#ScarlettWhite⁠

posts.caption:
Happy birthday Miuccia Prada. The designer turns 77 years old today. ⁠
⁠
Born in 1949, Miuccia Prada grew up in Milan, where she studied political science and was active in the feminist group Unione Donne Italiane. After studying mime at the Piccolo Teatro, she joined the family business in the mid-70s and met Patrizio Bertelli in 1978. Together, they launched Prada’s nylon backpack in 1984, followed by womenswear in 1988 and Miu Miu in 1993.⁠
⁠
For our Autumn/Winter 2021 issue alongside an interview with Susannah Frankel, the designer, for the first time, pulled from the Miu Miu archive to dress a cast of powerfully individual young people, who were photographed by Jamie Hawkesworth and styled by Katie Shillingford. Revisit the story at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
Photography by @jamie.hawkesworth⁠

posts.caption:
For almost three decades, Roe Ethridge has moved fluidly between fashion and fine art photography, shooting high-octane campaigns for brands including Balenciaga and staging conceptual exhibitions around the world. ⁠
⁠
His latest show and book, Rude in the Good Way, pushes his provocative visual experiments further, with Lindsay Lohan in a set-designed spoof of Miami, photographs of John Currin’s pornographic paintings from the early 2000s, and erotic portraits of Ethridge’s partner and collaborator, @falconshakleton. At the link in bio he speaks to fellow photographer @mark_peckmezian about the art of collaboration, with text by @violetc0nroy 📲⁠
⁠
Taken from the Spring/Summer 2026 issue of AnOther Magazine. ⁠
⁠
Photography by @mark_peckmezian

posts.caption:
@audemarspiguet and @swatch rewrite the rules of watchmaking with the ‘Royal Pop Collection,’ a hybrid watch-inspired design. A collaboration between two Swiss watchmakers — one rooted in luxury, the other in mass-market design — this unexpected partnership brings a fresh take on contemporary watch culture.
Specifically, the ‘Royal Pop Collection’ blends Audemars Piguet’s iconic Royal Oak with Swatch’s 1980s POP aesthetic, resulting in a bold and experimental design. It also features Swatch’s newly developed SISTEM51 movement. The price is from £335, and it will be available at selected Swatch stores from 16 May.
Ahead of the launch, public reactions are mixed — with some calling the design fresh, while others describe it as resembling a children’s key ring. What do you think of the ‘Royal Pop Collection’? Swipe right to see all the details.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @mickaelmtw @swatch @audemarspiguet @leatherlibraryy @ifuckinglovewatches
#AudemarsPiguet #Swatch #RoyalPop

posts.caption:
@fkatwigs is set to play Joséphine Baker in a new biopic. Joséphine Baker was a singer, dancer, actress, and a civil rights activist. She was also the first Black woman to be buried in the Panthéon, in recognition of her service to France through her work in the Resistance and her efforts in human rights and equality.
The role feels perfectly suited to FKA twigs, a performer and dancer herself, bringing Baker’s extraordinary life to the screen.
The film title has not yet been announced. It is set to begin filming later this year, directed by Maïmouna Doucouré, with the support of Baker’s sons, Jean-Claude Bouillon-Baker and Brian Bouillon-Baker.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow A2Z Fashion Magazine Now.

posts.caption:
Nicole Kidman shows off her leg to Karl Lagerfeld and Anna Wintour at the Costume Institute
Gala, May 2005
The 05 gala itself was important because it marked a major museum retrospective on Chanel, tracing the house from Coco Chanel to Lagerfeld’s reinvention of the brand.
The exhibition was heavily sponsored by Chanel and helped cement the Met Gala’s transition into the celebrity-fashion spectacle we know today.
Via @ le__dauphin
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Archived Runway on May 12, 2026 tagging @nicolekidman. May be an image of gown and text.

posts.caption:
DIY punk and 70s underground culture shaped Christophe Decarnin’s Spring/Summer 2011 Balmain collection.
Drawing from the anti-fashion attitude of figures like Sid Vicious and filtering it through late 2000s Balmain excess, Decarnin covered biker jackets, ripped tees, and distressed American flag tanks in layers of safety pins, studs, and hardware. Soundtracked by Frank Sinatra’s *My Way* before shifting into Sid Vicious’ version, the show balanced rebellion with luxury in a way that defined the era.
The collection was both celebrated and criticized for pushing “Balmainia” to its extreme, but it became one of the defining images of early 2010s fashion. Looking back now, the skinny silhouettes, sharp shoulders, distressed denim, and indie sleaze energy feel unmistakably 2010s-coded.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Archived Runway on May 12, 2026 tagging @balmain, and @welcome.jpeg. May be an image of text that says 'ARCHIVED ARCHIVEDRUNWAY RUNWAY Balmain's S Excessive Use Of Safety Pins During Their Spring 2011 Show'.

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Over her stellar 20-year career, @phoebejtonkin has captivated audiences. The Sydney-born, New York-based actor began acting as a teenager playing a mermaid in the series ‘H2O: Just Add Water,’ and has since achieved a dynamic array of dramatic parts, a stint in the director’s chair and a new role producing and starring in the adaptation of crime thriller, ‘The Dark Lake’. While she’s relishing the collaborative process, the actor and May 2026 cover star tells BAZAAR, acting remains her first love.“I feel really lucky to be invited into the collaborative process so early on in a project. Usually, as an actor, you turn up and you’re told, ‘This is what your set looks like, these are your fellow actors’. And with this, I get to be there from the inception of the project and be part of these decisions.”
Through the link in bio, Phoebe Tonkin reflects on her career and attraction to robust roles that “lean into the complexities of being a woman.”
Phoebe Tonkin wears @tiffanyandco HardWear collection for the May 2026 issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.caption:
“I’m attracted to things that are authentic and grounded,” @phoebejtonkin tells BAZAAR of her latest role in the cross-genre ‘Two Years Later’, a post-pandemic romantic dramedy set in Brisbane. Tonkin plays Emily, a woman with a complicated love and family life who sets out on a series of dates with Ryan, played by Brenton Thwaites, to work out if they’re destined to marry. “There was something quite relatable and ordinary about these two people who are in their 30s and trying to navigate dating, coming out of a pandemic, all the isolation and loneliness that they’ve been dealing with. And I just thought it was really real,” she shares. It was this funny, quirky love story drama that you don’t get to do often.”
Continue reading Phoebe Tonkin’s cover story through the link in bio.

Chalamet’s appointment was not only a splashy moment for the brand but seemed to generate authentic buzz: According to Launchmetrics, Chalamet’s campaign announcement earned one of the highest media impact values of any celebrity fragrance contract in recent history — behind A$AP Rocky for Gucci Guilty in 2024, and ahead of Rihanna’s blockbuster announcement as the new face of Dior J’adore.

posts.caption:
New luxury store openings last year fell to their lowest level since 2020, according to property adviser Savills. More than half of all openings were concentrated in a group of roughly 30 so-called alpha cities — including London, New York, Paris, Dubai, Milan and Los Angeles.
While luxury store openings increased slightly in Europe and the US, Asia’s share of new stores has slipped. The pullback has been most pronounced in tier-two and tier-three cities in China, which for years drove the bulk of global expansion. In 2025, China accounted for just 22 percent of new luxury store openings, down from 40 percent two years earlier.
Ferragamo, which has shown signs of emerging from a prolonged downturn, plans to close about 70 stores while opening roughly half that number in better locations. Most of the closures will be in China, with the group increasingly relying on e-commerce to serve demand in smaller cities.
BoF’s Eric Sylvers (@ericsylvers) explores how labels are rethinking their retail networks as they seek better returns amid a prolonged industry downturn. Read more #linkinbio

posts.caption:
For over a century, Saks Fifth Avenue represented a manifestation of American aspiration — a luxury icon whose flagship on New York’s Fifth Avenue served as a vital crossroads for the global fashion industry. But even the most storied institutions are not infallible. On Jan. 13, the newly formed Saks Global — parent company of Saks, Neiman Marcus, and Bergdorf Goodman — filed for court-supervised restructuring.
Saks Global’s crisis was largely self-inflicted. The acquisition of Neiman Marcus, coupled with slow payments to vendors resulted in a deepening inventory crisis.
As debt obligations mounted and cash reserves dwindled, Saks fell further behind on vendor payments, prompting suppliers to freeze shipments. Without new merchandise to sell, revenue plummeted, trapping the retailer in a terminal liquidity crunch. It was caught up in a downward spiral that left its industry reputation in tatters.
Now, just four months into Chapter 11, the company’s new CEO Geoffroy van Raemdonck is leading a turnaround effort to salvage its reputation and restore trust with its customers and the wider industry.
In this special episode of The BoF Podcast, BoF’s retail editor Cathaleen Chen (@kittykewlcat) and Imran Amed (@imranamed) sit down with van Raemdonck to unpack his plans for a big turnaround.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheBoFPodcast
📷 Saks Global

posts.caption:
Beauty retail is no longer linear.
Today’s consumers move fluidly between TikTok, Amazon, Sephora counters, brand websites and AI-powered search — discovering, researching and purchasing across multiple touchpoints in a single journey.
This shift accelerates the need for a more connected strategy, moving beyond treating each platform, marketplace and touchpoint in isolation.
In this playbook, Beauty Retail in the Age of Connected Commerce — created in partnership with Front Row — we explore how beauty brands can turn connected data, marketplaces and media into measurable growth.
The playbook features expert insights and strategies from Front Row’s Rina Yashayeva and Christopher Skinner, alongside best-in-class strategies from @theouai, @mynuface and more. #linkinbio.

posts.caption:
Saks Global may be emerging out of bankruptcy earlier than planned — a confirmation hearing is scheduled for early June — but new chief executive Geoffroy van Raemdonck is well aware that financial repair is only half the battle as he aims to get the luxury behemoth back on track.
The seasoned fashion executive, who helped steward Neiman Marcus Group out of its own bankruptcy in 2020, is now in the midst of a high stakes charm offensive aimed at mending fractured relationships with brands large and small after years of late payments, unfulfilled terms and broken trust. A number of independent brands will not be paid for money previously owed to them.
In an exclusive, in-depth interview with BoF on his post-bankruptcy strategy, van Raemdonck didn’t sugarcoat the damage done to brand partners in recent years. Explore the full conversation #linkinbio
✍️ @kittykewlcat, @ImranAmed

posts.caption:
Is ethnicwear in its cool girl era? For years, Indian luxury operated on a very specific fantasy: heavy bridalwear, hyper-traditional styling, formality as status. The clothes are beautiful, but they often felt designed for spectacle first & the wearer second, tied to the ‘heaviest lehenga wins’ Olympics.
But Indian fashion has shifted significantly over the last decade, especially among Gen Z consumers, who still want to dress Indian but on their own terms. At the same time, social media accelerated trend cycles & made Indian fashion feel increasingly homogenised online. Every wedding started looking the same with Y2K rehash lehengas, corset blouses, same styling formulas pasted across social media & celeb wardrobes.
Gen Z consumers are increasingly rejecting both extremes: the stiffness of tradition & the monotony of trend culture. They want Indian fashion that feels culturally, stylistically, and socially relevant.
While many Indian luxury labels continued selling grandeur & old-world opulence, @payalsinghal built an aesthetic around a much lighter & relaxed idea of glamour. Printed lehengas, playful tassels, tie-back cholis, fluid shararas, cape sets, softer silhouettes. In many ways, she helped shape the design language that now dominates new gen ethnicwear.
The appeal also lies in how she balances these current sensibilities with the artistry & craftsmanship that remain deeply rooted in Indian crafts. This comes through clearly in ‘Shahnameh’, a collection inspired by the Persian epic that merges regal references, cultural memory, and hard to miss artisanal details.
What makes it particularly appealing to Gen Z is that the clothes leave room for interpretation. The pieces do not feel prescriptive. That flexibility matters to a generation that sees fashion as personal branding.
That is why @payalsinghal resonates so strongly with fashion girls right now. It understands the emotional relationship young women currently have with their desi fits.

"Seaworld Venice" has already captured the attention of many hundreds of people who lined up for a preview of the Austrian pavilion at this year's Venice Biennale.
The popular show by performance artist Florentina Holzinger and curator Nora-Swantje Almes asks viewers to rethink patriarchal systems "that currently control our lives," according to Almes.
More than half a million people are expected to visit the monumental international art fair, which opens to the public Saturday, over the next six months to see the work of over 100 artists and 99 nations, spread across 31 permanent national pavilions and a series of other exhibition spaces.

posts.caption:
Generally speaking, you can't sit on the art in a museum. But in one gallery of the Museum of Contemporary Art Chicago (@mcachicago) — which is currently staged to resemble a karaoke bar complete with a disco ball, stage and jukebox — three plastic chairs, upholstered with the face of Puerto Rican superstar @badbunnypr, are waiting for you to rest between songs.
@edrasoto's "BB Chairs" — outfitted in bootleg fabrics featuring the singer with sunglasses and buzz cuts — have been a tongue-in-cheek nod to both the plastic white chair ubiquitous to the island and the performer's deep connection to his home.
"I had this idea a whole year before I made them," she said. "I was doubting myself. I was thinking maybe this is too on the nose."
But friends excitedly reached out to Soto when Bad Bunny released the now history-making, @grammys Award-winning album "DeBÍ TiRAR MáS FOToS." The album cover featured two empty white lawn chairs — an evocative symbol of home and belonging in Puerto Rico — and they had significance to Soto's practice, too.
"The furniture that I grew up with was wicker and plastic," she explained. "I asked myself what my chair would look like if I was making a chair." She said she couldn't relate to high-end materials, and began thinking about the fantasy of luxury in both the practice of upholstery and the colorful, if culturally inaccurate, images associated with the tropics.
📸 : Courtesy the artist/E G Schempf/Courtesy Kemper Museum of Contemporary Art
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by CNN Style on May 07, 2026 tagging @mcachicago, and @edrasoto. May be pop art of armchair, rocking chair, poster, chair and text that says 'THE BAD BUNNY CHAIRS TAKING OVER THE ART WORLD CNN'.

posts.caption:
The return of @bts.bighitofficial in March, nearly four years after they went on hiatus for mandatory military service, has highlighted a key question for the K-pop industry: What comes next?
This current generation has arguably grander ambitions – some of which beg the question: Does K-pop need to be Korean at all?
For instance, @blackpinkofficial's recently released mini-album "Deadline" is almost entirely in English. And new groups are debuting in other parts of the world, some with members that have no ties to South Korea.
"There's more global collaboration, and work within the K-pop world to become globalized, within every generation," said Danny Chung, the songwriter behind several major Blackpink hits.
Some of this is happening in other musical genres, too, with social media increasingly shaping how we consume and produce music. But it's especially true of K-pop, which is "ever-changing," said Chung, who also works in the Artists and Repertoire (A&R) team for record company The Black Label.
Experts say this fluidity could fuel the industry's aim to expand beyond borders and neat categorization.
"It's a forever moving target to try to define the genre of K-pop," Chung said.
📸 : Kim Hong-Ji/Pool/Getty Images/Christopher Polk/Billboard/Andrew Caballero-Reynolds/AFP

posts.caption:
In “Portrait of a Lady” for LOVE Magazine Spring/Summer 2016, Tim Walker pushes fashion photography into something stranger, more theatrical, and almost surreal. The editorial plays with exaggerated proportions, distorted femininity, heavy styling, and absurd details, yet never loses its elegance. Every image feels carefully constructed but still spontaneous, balancing humour, tension, and beauty at the same time.
What makes the story so compelling is the way it embraces excess without becoming chaotic. Oversized silhouettes, exaggerated bust shapes, eccentric props, and dramatic beauty looks turn the editorial into something between fashion fantasy and performance art. Inspired by the work of painter John Currin, Walker creates images that feel playful, slightly uncomfortable, and visually unforgettable.
The cast moves through these exaggerated scenes with complete seriousness, which gives the story its impact. Anna Cleveland, Eva Herzigova, Kiki Willems, Lexi Boling, Julie Hoomans, Molly Constable, and India Salvor Menuez each bring a different energy to the editorial, making every frame feel like its own strange character study.
Like much of Walker’s work, the story rejects minimalism completely. Instead, it celebrates imagination, visual storytelling, and the idea that fashion photography can still feel unexpected.
Editorial Credits
Editorial: Portrait of a Lady
Publication: LOVE Magazine
Season: Spring/Summer 2016
Photography: Tim Walker
Styling: Katie Grand
Makeup: Miranda Joyce
Hair: Shon
Cast: Anna Cleveland, Eva Herzigova, Kiki Willems, Lexi Boling, Julie Hoomans, Molly Constable, India Salvor Menuez
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 13, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, poster and text that says 'Nobody creates Dream Worlds like Tim Walker. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
“Fetching is Your Dior” from 1976 remains one of the boldest fashion campaigns ever produced.
Photographed by Chris von Wangenheim, the Dior jewelry advertisement captured model Lisa Taylor alongside a snarling Doberman, its jaws wrapped around her diamond covered wrist. The image was glamorous, provocative, and unsettling all at once, exactly the kind of visual language that began defining fashion photography in the second half of the 1970s.
Rather than presenting luxury as soft or untouchable, the campaign framed it as something sharper and more aggressive. Heavy jewelry, glossy makeup, sculpted hair, and dramatic poses gave the images a sense of control and confidence, while the presence of the dog introduced tension and unpredictability. It blurred the line between elegance and danger in a way that felt completely new at the time.
The campaign also reflected a larger shift happening in fashion imagery during the era. By the late 1970s, advertising was becoming more cinematic, sexually charged, and emotionally provocative. Photographers like Chris von Wangenheim moved away from traditional polished beauty photography and introduced darker, more psychological narratives that would go on to influence decades of fashion editorials and luxury campaigns.
Nearly fifty years later, “Fetching is Your Dior” still feels strikingly modern, not only because of its styling, but because of its attitude. It captured the moment fashion stopped trying to look merely beautiful and started wanting to look powerful.
Campaign Credits
Campaign: Fetching is Your Dior
Brand: Dior
Year: 1976
Photographer: Chris von Wangenheim
Model: Lisa Taylor
Makeup : Joey Mills
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 12, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, makeup, magazine, poster and text that says 'This is what Dior campaigns looked like in the 70s. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
Long before fashion openly embraced fluidity and self-reinvention, Pete Burns was already challenging ideas of beauty, gender, and identity through the way he presented himself. As the frontman of Dead or Alive, he became one of the most recognizable figures of the 1980s — not only because of the music, but because of his unmistakable image.
Heavy makeup, sharp silhouettes, bold hair, leather, lace, Pete Burns approached style as something personal and expressive rather than something defined by trends. His look constantly changed over the years, but it always felt authentic to him. That honesty is part of why he remains such an important figure in fashion and pop culture today.
What made Burns influential was the way he moved beyond traditional ideas of masculinity and beauty at a time when very few public figures did. He made individuality visible and showed that image could be used as a form of identity, creativity, and control.
Today, his influence can still be seen across fashion, beauty, and music culture. Many of the aesthetics and ideas that feel current now, androgyny, extreme self-styling, dark glamour, and self-created identity, were things Pete Burns embodied decades earlier.
More than anything, he represented the freedom to become whoever you wanted to be.
And nobody did it like him.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 11, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, hair, poster, magazine and text that says 'Pete Burns turned Rebellion into Power. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
Martin Schoeller makes everyone look equally exposed.
With „Portraits“ (2005–2026), Martin Schoeller expands his instantly recognizable close-up style into something more cinematic and psychologically charged. The series moves beyond the traditional celebrity portrait, placing actors, musicians, politicians, and cultural figures into carefully constructed situations that feel both controlled and strangely revealing.
What makes Schoeller’s work so distinctive is its consistency. The direct gaze, the centered composition, the hyper-detailed lighting, every subject is photographed with the same visual precision, whether it’s Barack Obama, Angela Merkel, Lady Gaga, or an anonymous face. That uniformity removes distraction and hierarchy, forcing the viewer to focus entirely on expression, texture, emotion, and presence.
At the same time, Portraits never feels cold. There’s humour in the exaggeration, tension in the stillness, and often a subtle absurdity running through the images. Some portraits feel vulnerable, others theatrical or unsettling, but all of them challenge the polished distance usually associated with celebrity photography.
Rather than documenting fame, Schoeller captures something more human: the strange space between public image and private identity. His portraits are sharp, confrontational, and impossible to look away from, not because of spectacle, but because of how much they reveal in the smallest details.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 10, 2026. May be an image of poster, magazine and text that says 'Fame disappears, in front of Schoeller's Camera. CULT'.

posts.caption:
A chaotic masterpiece of fashion theatre. Distorted beauty, exaggerated expressions, smudged eyes, and grotesque glamour collide in this unforgettable editorial.
Shot through Steven Meisel’s provocative lens, the story transforms couture into performance art: part cabaret, part nightmare, part punk fantasia. The models become living characters rather than polished mannequins, embodying a raw, almost unsettling energy that defined the fearless spirit of late-2000s editorials.
With dramatic close-ups, warped proportions, and heavily stylized makeup, every image feels cinematic and emotionally charged — blurring the line between beauty and madness. Galliano-era eccentricity, underground club culture, silent-film theatrics, and avant-garde fashion all merge into one surreal visual spectacle that could only belong to the golden age of fashion editorials.
“Performance”
Vogue Italia September 2009
Photographer: Steven Meisel
Fashion Editor / Stylist: Karl Templer
Hair: Julien d’Ys
Makeup: Pat McGrath
Manicure: Jin Soon Choi
Set Designer: Maru Howard
Models: Anna Jagodzinska, Ash Stymest, Christian Brylle, Coco Rocha, Jamie Bochert, Max Rogers, Meghan Collison, Sasha Pivovarova, Sessilee López, Simon Nessman, Viktoriya Sasonkina

posts.caption:
The Steve Madden ads in the 2000s had a really distinctive style that made them instantly recognizable. Most of the campaigns featured models with oversized heads and tiny bodies, creating an almost doll-like or cartoon-inspired look. The editing was playful and exaggerated, which matched the bold energy of early 2000s fashion perfectly.
The ads usually placed the models in everyday settings like city streets, bedrooms, rooftops, or suburban neighborhoods, but everything felt slightly surreal and stylized. Bright colors, dramatic shoes, heavy makeup, and exaggerated poses gave the campaigns a fun and edgy vibe without taking themselves too seriously.
A big focus was always on the shoes. Whether it was chunky platforms, heels, boots, or sandals, the footwear stood out as the center of the image. The playful proportions and unusual compositions made the products feel trendy, youthful, and full of personality.
Looking back, the campaigns capture the spirit of Y2K fashion really well — experimental, confident, slightly chaotic, and very visual. Even today, the ads still feel iconic because they had such a unique identity compared to other fashion campaigns of the time.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 08, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine, poster and text that says 'From the Archives: Steve Madden Ads in the 00s CULT®'.

posts.caption:
When photographer @ryanotoolecollett arrived in Mexico City in 2024, he didn’t intend to begin the project that would later become A Caged Dog Barks the Fiercest.⁠
⁠
Before he arrived in the country, however, Ryan began to examine how much of his own understanding of Mexico had been shaped by the media. These “subconscious assumptions” became central to the project, which looks at how people in Mexico City define themselves beyond the categories imposed on them from the outside.⁠
⁠
In the photo series, Ryan photographs more than 40 individuals from Mexico City and presents 30 of their stories side by side in English and Mexican Spanish. While the work is about identity, it resists offering a one-dimensional portrait of the city.⁠
⁠
Tap the link in bio to see more 🔗⁠
⁠
✍️ @tiiarna⁠
📸 @ryanotoolecollett⁠
⁠
#Dazed

posts.caption:
From Chappell Roan to Sarah Squirm to drag scenes across the US, clowning is experiencing a cultural revival. That’s especially true at CLOWN CULT, a beloved sold-out monthly variety show in Brooklyn.⁠
⁠
Three years after its founding, @clowncultclowncult has developed recurring characters, plotlines and mythology-adjacent notoriety. It’s also giving people a space to show up as the wildest versions of themselves. “Clowning is all about exaggerating parts of yourself that you want to exaggerate,” Giggles, a clown of several years, tells Dazed.⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, writer Chloe Yang takes us behind the scenes of a recent CLOWN CULT show in Bushwick, and unpacks the recent uptick in clown aesthetics 🔗 ⁠
⁠
📸 Julian Lazaro
posts.alt_text: Photo by Dazed Beauty on May 12, 2026. May be an image of dancing, fishnet stockings, poster, magazine, pole, night and text that says 'A hot, sweaty night with Brooklyn's young clowns'.

posts.caption: This is out of touch. After the Epstein files, this feels like a mockery. It has nothing to do with feminism or women’s self-determination. This is clearly made for the male gaze in an industry that is increasingly hypersexualizing many female mainstream artists, so it’s regardless of whether her choice was free or not. Feminism requires critical thinking, not simplistic conclusions. And now people on the internet are actually wondering if she’s okay, but not questioning her freedom or the entire history of the babydoll aesthetic in pop culture lol. There’s finally awareness. It’s not about blaming her, it’s about our damn context, which is very different from the 90s: we’re living through a backlash against women, so people want things to feel safe

posts.caption:
There is a raw, magnetic power in the quiet admission of needing the dark to reflect. In a world obsessed with overexposure, Dazed remains the ultimate platform for those of us who find our truth in the unlit corners of the soul. Chloe’s words resonate deeply—sometimes the most profound growth happens in the absence of an audience.
​As an independent artist who consistently creates from the stillness of the shadows, I celebrate this visual and intellectual sanctuary. It is a reminder that the light only reveals what we have already forged in the silence. Never stop pushing the boundaries of identity.
​Warm regards from the deepest shadows,
​Andrew Vamp

At Forum on KP Dialogue 2026, global leaders discussed how natural diamonds continue to face communication challenges despite their socio-economic and ethical contributions.
One major concern raised during the session was the absence of a unified industry-wide communication strategy — allowing competing narratives to dominate consumer conversations.
Speakers emphasized that storytelling, social media and transparency will play a major role in shaping the future perception of natural diamonds.
Because today, trust is built through communication.
Do you think the industry needs stronger storytelling? 👇

posts.caption:
Can natural diamonds become carbon neutral? 💎🌍
At KP Forum 2026, sustainability emerged as one of the industry’s biggest talking points.
ALROSA presented findings from a five-year study claiming kimberlite ore naturally absorbs and stores CO2 — with some mining operations described as carbon neutral and even carbon negative.
The session also introduced discussions around a possible future carbon-credit framework within the Kimberley Process.
As sustainability becomes increasingly important to consumers, the natural diamond industry appears to be entering a new era of conversation.
Would carbon-neutral claims influence consumer perception? 👇
#DiamondWorld #NaturalDiamonds #Sustainability #KPForum2026

posts.caption:
Credibility. Confidence. Compliance. 💎
These were the three pillars at the centre of the Forum on KP Dialogue held on May 10 during the Kimberley Process Intersessional 2026 in Mumbai.
From sustainability and traceability to consumer trust and transparency, the forum brought together global stakeholders to discuss the evolving future of the natural diamond industry.
As conversations around natural diamonds continue to evolve, one thing became clear:
The future of the industry will be built on trust.

posts.caption:
North Carolina fourth-grader Christian Mango, 10, submitted a school-assigned essay to Rep. Virginia Foxx(R-NC), arguing that electric car buyers should receive a tax credit because EVs may have a lower environmental impact than traditional cars. In a scathing response to the child, Foxx told him his teachers were “indoctrinating” him.
“Incidentally, please ask your teacher to explain propaganda to you,” Foxx wrote. “While I will never be able to know you, my guess is that your teachers will not give you a good educational experience and help you learn to think as they are too interested in indoctrinating you. How sad.” The rant wrapped with a plug to like and subscribe to Foxx’s socials and newsletter. Wow.
Christian’s mother, Emily Mango, said she is now “embarrassed” to call the ELEVEN-TERM congresswoman her representative, while Christian said he thought Foxx’s response was simply “wrong” since his school didn’t choose his topic.
Foxx is currently seeking re-election for a 12th term and has been endorsed by, you guessed it, Trump. Another day, another argument for term and age limits, ffs
#termlimits #news #reels #threads #viral

posts.caption:
On Monday, the White House hosted a meeting on America’s so-called “fertility crisis,” during which Trump crowned himself the “father of fertility” and bragged that he learned “everything about fertility” in just four minutes. Truly reassuring from the man who once suggested injecting bleach to treat COVID.
During the meeting, RFK Jr. citelessly claimed that teenagers today just don’t have enough sperm. “This is a threat not only to our economy, but to our national security,” he warned, blaming everything from obesity and pesticides to a concept he vaguely defined as America’s “toxic soup” (??)
Dr. Oz also chimed in to declare the nation “underbabied,” explaining that people are either not having children or having fewer than they want.
After the parade of bizarre statements, critics online pointed out the glaring reality missing from the administration’s panic: skyrocketing healthcare costs, rising maternal mortality rates, shrinking abortion access, unaffordable childcare, stagnant wages, and attacks on vaccines likely have far more to do with declining birth rates than whatever “toxic soup” Worm Brain Jr. is rambling about.
“You want women to have more kids?” one person wrote on Threads. “Quit infringing on their bodily autonomy. Make unrestricted health care, child care affordable. Add paid [maternity] leave, equal pay, and make the cost of living affordable.”
Maybe the men spiraling about teenage sperm and claiming to understand fertility after a few minutes shouldn’t be the ones shaping reproductive policy… and maybe they could solve the “crisis” by starting with a basic understanding of why so many people can’t afford to have kids in the first place.
#news #reels #fertility #healthcare #thetestaments

posts.caption:
Did you see Olivia Rodrigo’s ‘Drop Dead’ music video where she’s frolicking around Versailles wearing a Chloé blouse as pajamas? Did that look bother you?
For some viewers, the ‘babydoll’ look, owing to its name as well as its association with children’s apparel, called to mind the pedophilia which recently came to light in the Epstein Files. They’ve called Rodrigo’s look “baby cosplay” and have accused her of “normalising” pedophilic imagery.
But there are two outfits in ‘Drop Dead,’ one of which closely resembles a white Loris Azzaro dress formerly worn by Jane Birkin. This reference is important because Birkin wasn’t only iconic for wearing babydoll styles, but was a leader in a 60s style movement which freed women from cinched waists and allowed them the comfort of non-restrictive silhouettes. Perhaps Rodrigo anticipated the critique she is now receiving and was offering a Birkin as a counterpoint?
As well, Rodrigo confirmed that she found inspiration for her babydoll look — which she has doubled down on since the release of ‘Drop Dead’ — in such punky style mavens as Courtney Love and Kat Bjelland who wore the look in the 90s as a way to hold a middle finger up to establishment beauty standards. Their interpretation of the babydoll, which ran parallel to the riot grrrl movement and musicians like Kathleen Hanna and Kim Gordon, was dubbed “kinderwhore.”
Olivia Rodrigo, though, is a pop star whose work doesn’t compromise mainstream appeal despite channeling the ingredients of anti-establishment style. Can she pull off the “kinderwhore” look as a form of defiance? That looks to be the debate which reached a fever pitch this week when she wore another babydoll dress at Spotify’s Billions Club concert in Barcelona.
At its core, the debate is about the fine line between protecting women from exploitation and policing women’s autonomy, especially as it relates to the clothes they choose to wear. And with big pop-stars, this debate can be tricky; At what point are we oppressively scrutinizing what an intelligent, grown woman has chosen to wear, vs. at what point are we criticizing how the pop-culture icon has been styled for the sake of marketing?

posts.caption:
Tennessee Republicans just passed a new congressional map that redraws Memphis’ majority-Black Democratic district, a move critics say is designed to help the GOP keep control of the House.
Republicans claim the redistricting reflects population shifts, but opponents are calling BS, arguing the map is designed to dilute Black voting power.  “You cannot take a majority Black city, fracture its voting power and then tell us race has nothing to do with it,” Sen. London Lamar said.
The vote sparked major protests inside the state Capitol, with demonstrators filling the galleries alongside Democratic lawmakers. State Rep. Justin Jones burned a Confederate flag during the legislative session, while State Rep. Justin J. Pearson confronted THP troopers as they cleared protesters out. State Sen. Charlane Oliver stood on her desk holding a “No Jim Crow” banner before Republicans abruptly ended the session. Gov. Bill Lee signed the map into law shortly after.
The new districts come just days after SCOTUS weakened key Voting Rights Act protections, making Tennessee the first state to redraw congressional maps under the new ruling.
#news #tennessee #reels #threads #viral

posts.caption:
The fatal outbreak of hantavirus aboard a cruise ship sailing the Atlantic reportedly may have been contracted during a Dutch couple’s bird-watching trip at a landfill aka people playing in literal trash 🙃
Hantavirus is a rare, deadly disease primarily carried by infected rodents. So far, three passengers on the Oceanwide Expeditions cruise ship have died, with at least four others showing symptoms, according to the World Health Organization (WHO).
A KLM flight attendant who was in contact with a now deceased passenger is also showing symptoms and is being tested for hantavirus, authorities in the Netherlands have confirmed.
On Thursday, Oceanwide issued an update saying 30 guests disembarked the ship on April 24, nearly two weeks after the first passenger died on April 11. According to Oceanwide, the ship was carrying 88 passengers and 61 crew members.
Despite the outbreak, WHO insists there’s no need to panic, saying “the risk to the global population from this event as low.”
The US completed its withdrawal from the WHO in Jan 2026, following an Executive Order signed by President Trump on his first day in office last year. Oh, he also gutted the CDC, but no need to worry since RFK is at the helm now ::screams into pillow::
#hantavirus #threads #meme #dump #news

posts.caption:
Creativity might be your bread and butter but finance is your marmalade. The Devil Wears Prada 2 may be centred around fashion media/publishing, but underneath the gloss and cerulean callbacks sits a far more interesting horror story: the corporate takeover of creativity. The film captures the exact moment we’re living through, where finance bros, CMOs, growth leads, data evangelists, and PowerPoint gladiators have fully entered the teams chat and decided they are now the tastemakers too.
For years, editors bullied the suits. They were named, “shirt-pant-belt!”. The finance teams were the boring people downstairs saying no to fantasy shoots, sixteen-page fashion stories, impossible set builds, and editors flying first class to Paris for “research” or fashion weeks. But the revenge arc is complete now. The suits won. The CMOs/marketing heads/MDs who control the flow of cash control the narrative, the guest list approvals, the covers, the partnerships, the talent, the visibility, the the “cultural moments”, and subsequently the aesthetics themselves. Suddenly everyone with a quarterly target thinks they’re a creative director LOL. Because the old-school legacy editor is basically dead. That mythical, terrifying, cigarillo-smoking, culturally omniscient editor figure who dictated tastes from atop a magazine floor no longer exists in the same way. Sad!
Everyone is a “digital editor” now. Everyone has analytics dashboards open. Everyone is chasing engagement graphs, completion rates, retention curves, SEO juice, virality, affiliate conversions, and “share of voice”. Every publication, influencer, creator, luxury house, meme page, founder, stylist, podcaster, and celebrity is screaming into the same collapsing attention economy hoping to hold eye contact for three seconds before the next scroll. DISCUSS ☕️👀

posts.caption:
Remember when traditional print magazines used to do the …quintessential “Must-Have” lists?? Yeah that, but with the new Stella McCartney x H&M. She is back with the brand for the second time (gotta give!), and now with 20 more years of experience under her belt, she has come with callbacks to signature pieces across her career. Think archive gone accessible 😌🤏🏾
Starting from the re-edition of the iconic DIY “Rock Royalty” tops that Stella and Liv Tyler wore to the 1999 MET Gala. A fashion nerd’s instant check-out. And the cut-out bejewelled jeans similar to her main line? A no-brainer purchase! The rest of the collection, dresses, bags, tops, etc., aligns with the same synergy of staples elevated by the sensibility of fashion’s original IT girl. With all that to choose from, I have made it easy for y’all and picked my favourites off the list. 💅💅

posts.caption:
Lights, camera, @billieeilish.
At just 24 years old, the 10-time Grammy winner and two-time Oscar winner has existed in the public consciousness for a decade now. But this May, a new concert documentary attempts to capture her star power and stage presence in a new light—through the eyes of legendary director James Cameron, no less. Eilish never thought she’d make another documentary after 2021’s ‘Billie Eilish: The World’s a Little Blurry,’ which closely documented her adolescence and the making of her sensational 2019 debut album. “I’m so private about my actual life now,” Eilish tells ELLE, but when the ‘Avatar’ filmmaker came calling, she knew she had to pick up—and co-direct the film alongside him.
In ELLE’s May 2026 cover story, Eilish opens up about collaborating with Cameron, how her personal and professional relationship with her brother @finneas has evolved, and her activist spirit: “I make art and music to reach people, to help people.” Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Here’s some big news: in the UK, @vinted is now more popular than some of the most sought after fast fashion brands. This is major 🤯⁠
⁠
As @allthingsfashiontech pointed out recently, #Vinted has become the third largest retailer in the UK, all of which is emphasised by how the platform’s sales keep rising and rising 📈⁠
⁠
There may be obvious concerns around overconsumption and secondhand clothing, but know that pre-owned clothing is a key player in circular fashion as it keeps clothing that already exists within the system. Any clothing that is diverted from landfills is a win ✅⁠
⁠
What are your thoughts on Vinted’s popularity? Are you a fan of secondhand fashion? Share in the comments, then learn more about vintage and thrifting at the link ↑⁠

posts.caption:
@chloessevigny is the real deal. For three decades, Sevigny has cut a singular path, both on screen and in fashion, building a body of work that feels as unexpected as it does authentically her own. In Bazaar’s May 2026 issue, the actor talks about her next move—“The Five-Star Weekend,” a drama series adapted from the Elin Hilderbrand novel in which she stars alongside Jennifer Garner. It is not the kind of project that one would immediately associate with Sevigny, but that’s precisely why it also makes sense; she’ll never fit neatly into any box. “My agents sent it to me, and I was like, ‘This is too mainstream. My fans are not going to like it,’” Sevigny says, “but I really feel there’s really something for everybody.”
-

posts.caption: Absolutely nothing wrong with a babydoll dress...BUT the particular frilly bloomers exposed n high socks n kiddish styling is problematic AF. We don't live in isolation. Where is the punk grittiness? Where is the chic sophistication? Where is the cute sexiness? Where is the cut and construction of the babydoll that's supposed to still make it look like it's for an adult body? Why isn't it an actual baby doll dress n not a ruffled bust minimising tiered top worn on frilly bloomers???? And most importantly why is coquette trending amongst 11-15yos? You'll lack critical thinking skills & nuanced understanding of fashion evolution.

posts.caption:
If you want to know what’s happening at S/DOUBLE, @shawnstussy low-key streetwear label, you still have to look to Australia. After quietly shutting the brand down in 2016, the Stüssy founder revived it years later with little more than a handwritten mural outside Globe’s Australian HQ. Now in its seventh season, the rebooted label continues to favor old-school rollout tactics over social media hype, debuting collections on billboards and city walls before they ever hit the internet. Recent projections across Melbourne showed Stussy hand-drawing S/DOUBLE’s signature curlycue logo, while the seasonal campaign, shot by his son Tate Stussy, places the 71-year-old designer directly in frame.⁠
⁠
The clothes follow the same familiar rhythm: relaxed workwear, boxy graphic tees, plaid shirting, and easy wide-leg silhouettes stamped with Stussy’s unmistakable graffiti-style graphics. It’s classic SoCal-by-way-of-streetwear fare from the designer who helped define the genre in the first place. But despite renewed attention, S/DOUBLE remains Australia-exclusive, backed by Globe founders Peter and Stephen Hill. Stussy seems perfectly happy keeping it that way. As he recently wrote on Instagram: “This is what 71 laps gets you... freedom. [I’m] doing what I want.”
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on May 12, 2026 tagging @highsnobiety. May be an image of one or more people, people smoking, eyewear, magazine and text that says 'STÜSSY'S FOUNDER IS BACK TO MAKING STREETWEAR FOR HIMSELF H'.

posts.caption:
@supremenewyork joins forces with @s4lem for a collection that pulls heavily from the group’s unmistakable aesthetic. ⁠
⁠
The lineup includes a vintage-inspired stadium jacket, a zip-up hoodie, graphic tees, a New Era fitted, and socks, all wrapped in the kind of washed graphics and muted tones that feel synonymous with SALEM’s world. Between the sportswear references and SALEM’s long-standing influence on underground culture, the collection feels somewhat like a mix of tour merch, skatewear, and pure early-2010s nostalgia.⁠
⁠
The collection is slated for release on May 14 and on May 16 in Asia.⁠
⁠
📷️: Supreme

posts.caption:
If you’ve been online at all in the last few months, you’ll likely be familiar with India’s buzziest new designer, @diyajoukani. Her first-ever video went viral, which led to about a thousand more, and she’s since become known online as “that cool girl from India.” But honestly, she’s so much more than that.
A self-taught, former stylist who quit her job overnight and now designs for her own label, Joukani’s trajectory is anything but linear. From the beginning, she knew her talent was somewhat wasted in working for someone else, and thanks to her self-proclaimed “delusion”, she knew that whatever she did on her own was going to work out.
Alongside designing the clothing she wanted to see, she’s indirectly spotlighted the bustling community around her, which, for those who don’t know, is actively made up of her own tailors, friends and family members.
As Joukani’s career continues to blow up in the best way, Hypebae’s managing editor @naviahluwalia caught up with her for a conversation about her journey so far, her desire to preserve traditional craftsmanship and how she plans to bring New York Fashion Week to the streets of Bandra.
Images: @indiabharadwaj

posts.caption:
Even though @apc_paris just dropped its Brain Dead collaboration 🔥, we’re already looking forward to its next “interaction” with fragment design by @fujiwarahiroshi ⚡️⁠
⁠
Across a range of garments and accessories, Fujiwara’s knack for cinematic influences, and both brands’ affinity for denim are expressed with subtlety: a jacket silhouette inspired by Charlie Chaplin’s ‘Modern Times’ and an homage to Paul Newman in 'Cool Hand Luke', with trousers finished by a rubber stripe at the leg. ⁠
⁠
The stripe detail also lends itself to their interpretation of the “Canadian Tuxedo” (a common term for denim-on-denim jacket-trouser pairings), mirroring the taped detailing of traditional tuxedo pants. Each of the deep indigo pieces features a high contrast emblem combining fragment’s lightning bolt and A.P.C.’s guitar icon into one logo.⁠
⁠
In the wider collection, raw denim pieces are accompanied by white and indigo cotton tees, hoodies, and chinos, each featuring the collaborative wordmarks. Standout accessories include a raw denim hat and a tote bag to complete the look!⁠
⁠
The A.P.C. x fragment collection is set for release on May 19, 2026.⁠
⁠
Photo: A.P.C. x fragment⁠

posts.caption:
The quietest looks are always the loudest statements, and Avneet Kaur has known this for a while now. 🌸
Draped in the deep, unhurried cool of monochromatic indigo, she doesn’t chase the moment. She sets the pace for it. Denim-on-denim, lace against leather, coastal light and shadow, every element dialled into a frequency that’s entirely her own.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this isn’t just a fashion story. It’s a portrait of a generation that leads with composure, one that finds its power not in the loudest room, but in the stillest one. ✨
The industry moves fast. Avneet doesn’t rush. And somehow, she’s always exactly where the moment needs her to be.
Cool, collected, and completely in control, this is the indigo hour. And it belongs to her.

posts.caption:
Mai Tera on May 13? I'm a man. I have written a letter to my future wife. Dear future wife,
I never thought I would do such a "cheesy" thing, but here I am. I usually don't pen down my thoughts because it feels overwhelming, but I am trying today.
You must be thinking, why now? I want to put this out there because I am ready to meet you. I am tired of walking this path alone and I would be happy to have you by my side. I don't believe in cosmic forces, but I do believe in destiny—and if my words are true, I will find you. Maybe I am being selfish, but I want this reality.
We are faceless, nameless strangers right now. But one day, we are going to be each other's soulmates. One day, we will bare our souls to each other completely. I will know what you did when you were 5, what mistakes you made, how you learned, what made you strong, what makes you smile, and when you shed tears. I will be fortunate to know these little things. Just thinking about it makes me smile. Eventually, these subtle things will make me fall in love with you.
We are not there yet, but we will be.
I am chasing my goals and I am passionate about everything I do. I aspire to be an author one day when I am old. My main interests are reading, writing poetry, and traveling. Maybe I will write poems for you one day. (I love reading everything except romance books).
The traits I value are intelligence, kindness, empathy, and a passion for life.
I am hoping that we start talking soon. ❤️

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

Tessuti italiani, manifattura rigorosamente inglese. Una scelta che non è mai solo una scelta: l’azzurro appartiene da tempo alla grammatica stilistica della principessa del Galles, una tonalità rassicurante che comunica continuità. Il resto segue la stessa logica: body bianco Holland Cooper, orecchini di perle, l’anello con zaffiro di Lady Diana. Nulla è lasciato al caso. Non lo è mai. posts.caption:
@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Today, the Croisette becomes fashion’s most glamorous runway. ⁠
⁠
As the #CannesFilmFestival returns to the French Riviera, the world’s biggest actors, filmmakers, and celebrities will gather for two weeks of cinematic brilliance, high glamour, and unforgettable red carpet moments. Since 1946, Cannes has celebrated the best in film—but over the decades, it’s also become the stage for some of fashion’s most iconic entrances. From sweeping couture to legendary jewels, the festival’s style legacy is nearly as storied as the films themselves. Here are some of our favorite moments from the festival's history. Tap the link in our bio for more. ⁠
⁠
Photos Getty Images
#cannesfilmfestival2026 #katemoss #bellahadid #fashionhistory

Boho lace, romantic florals, satin slips, and floaty babydoll silhouettes — consider it official: this is the summer of Chloé. The fashion set has spoken, and carefree boho glamour is back at the top of the moodboard.⁠

posts.caption:
This week, two of fashion’s biggest houses are taking their Cruise collections on the road: @Dior heads to Los Angeles, while @Gucci lands in New York. But what exactly is a Cruise show?⁠
⁠
Arriving each year around #CannesFilmFestival season—and often just as spectacular—Cruise collections have long occupied a unique space in fashion. The tradition began in the early 20th century, when Europe and America’s wealthiest travelers would flee winter for warmer destinations like the Mediterranean and the Caribbean. The problem? Stores were still stocked with heavy coats and winter wool.⁠
⁠
In 1919, #GabrielleChanel saw the gap and designed a small wardrobe of lightweight dresses and knitwear for women vacationing in Biarritz. Chic, practical, and ahead of its time, it marked the beginning of what would become Cruise dressing: fashion created for escape.⁠
⁠
By the mid-century, Cruise collections became synonymous with leisure, glamour, and jet-set sophistication. Today, they’ve evolved into some of the industry’s most ambitious spectacles—untethered from the traditional fashion calendar and staged everywhere from historic landmarks to far-flung destinations. More than a runway show, Cruise has become fashion’s excuse to dream bigger.⁠
⁠
Photos Getty Images ⁠
⁠
#Cruise2027 #CruiseRunways #Resort2027

posts.caption:
#IsabellaBlow wasn’t just a fashion editor—she was a visionary talent scout with an instinct for greatness before the world caught on. As mentor, muse, and unwavering champion, she helped launch the careers of @AlexanderMcQueen, @PhilipTreacy, @JeremyScott, Stella Tennant, and Sophie Dahl.⁠
⁠
In the early ’90s, Blow discovered the young Lee McQueen, encouraging him to embrace the more regal “Alexander” and guiding him as both confidante and creative catalyst long before he became the celebrated head designer of @Givenchy. Together, Blow and McQueen became fashion’s inseparable "enfants terribles"—two extraordinary forces who forever changed the industry.⁠
⁠
A film exploring the pair’s extraordinary relationship, 'Wild Bird,' starring Olivia Colman and Russell Tovey, is currently in early development.⁠
⁠
Photos Getty Images⁠
⁠
#AlexanderMcQueen #FashionHistory #McQueen

posts.caption:
For nearly four decades, Anna Wintour didn’t just lead Vogue, she reshaped the entire fashion industry.
From transforming magazine covers in the late 1980s to turning the Met Gala into fashion’s biggest global stage, Wintour built an influence that extended far beyond publishing. Known for her signature bob, dark sunglasses, and uncompromising standards, she became one of the most powerful figures in modern fashion.
Even after stepping down as Vogue’s editor-in-chief in 2025, Anna Wintour continues to lead as Condé Nast’s global editorial director, proving her impact on fashion and media remains unmatched. (biography.com)
#Luxurious #MetGala

posts.caption:
Bridgerton isn’t just a romantic period drama, it’s one of Netflix’s most lavish productions. The series reportedly carries a staggering budget of around $168 million, placing it among the most expensive shows created by the streaming giant.
Each episode is estimated to cost around $7 million, funding the show’s extravagant costumes, grand sets, and cinematic storytelling that recreates Regency-era London. The scale of production is what gives the series its signature opulence and visual richness.
The spending doesn’t stop at sets and wardrobes. Reports also reveal impressive cast salaries, with some actors and voice talents earning up to $1 million for their roles, reflecting the show’s massive global success.
From lavish balls to royal romances, Bridgerton proves that luxury storytelling comes with a royal price tag and Netflix is willing to pay for it.
Images: Courtesy of Netflix
#Netflix #Luxurious

posts.caption:
When you’re Cristiano Ronaldo, a standard G-Wagon simply isn’t enough. The football icon has reportedly added a $2 million custom Mercedes-AMG G63 Cabriolet to his ever-growing hypercar collection, and it’s as over-the-top as you’d expect.
Built by Dubai-based luxury tuner Refined Marques, the ultra-exclusive SUV features a convertible soft-top design, rear suicide doors, CR7 detailing, and a bold yellow-and-blue finish inspired by Ronaldo’s club, Al-Nassr. Only 20 examples are expected to exist worldwide.
Powered by a twin-turbo V8 producing 577 horsepower, the one-off creation perfectly matches Ronaldo’s larger-than-life image, blending luxury, performance, and pure spectacle into one of the wildest G-Wagons ever built.
#Luxurious #Ronaldo #CristianoRonaldo

posts.caption:
Watch culture just entered a new era. The highly anticipated Audemars Piguet x Swatch collaboration has officially arrived, introducing the “Royal Pop” collection — a bold lineup of bioceramic pocket watches inspired by the iconic Royal Oak and Swatch’s playful Pop era.
Featuring eight vibrant colorways, Royal Oak-inspired octagonal bezels, Petite Tapisserie dials, and Swatch’s reworked hand-wound SISTEM51 movement with a 90-hour power reserve, the collection blends high horology with pop culture in a way the industry has never seen before.
Priced from $400 and launching exclusively in selected Swatch stores worldwide, the release is already generating MoonSwatch-level hype, with queues reportedly forming before launch day itself.

posts.caption:
Audi has offered the first glimpse inside its upcoming flagship Q9 SUV ahead of its global debut this July, and the cabin makes one thing clear: the brand is pushing deeper into ultra-luxury territory.
Positioned above the Q7, the all-new Q9 features a lounge-like three-row interior with six and seven-seat layouts, premium leather finishes, matte wood trims, ambient lighting, and a panoramic glass roof with smart dimming technology. Audi is also introducing power-operated doors, a 4D Bang & Olufsen sound system with seat-integrated vibration technology, and one of the brand’s most spacious cabins yet.
Set to rival luxury giants like the BMW X7 and Mercedes-Benz GLS, the Audi Q9 is shaping up to become the most advanced and luxurious SUV in Audi’s lineup so far.
#Luxurious #Audi

#ManifestCover
In Manifest’s May-June cover interview, Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth) reflects on grief, love, and the kind of resilience that quietly reshapes a person over time. Beneath the poise is a life shaped by movement, loss, and emotional inheritance, experiences that continue to inform the way she understands connection and presence.
“You don’t know what life will throw at you. But as long as you’re there for each other, that’s what matters. It’s about support and presence more than anything else,” she shares.

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
There is a quiet power to Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth). One that doesn’t seek attention, yet holds it effortlessly.
For Manifest’s May–June cover, she steps into an imaginative world where temple jewellery meets contemporary silhouettes, blurring the lines between memory and modernity. She embodies a woman in transition, grounded, luminous, and yet constantly becoming.
“I’m not very good at describing myself,” she says, almost in passing.
Yet, in the same breath, her choices reveal a quiet clarity of intent. “I don’t want to isolate my character. I need to understand the whole world it exists in.”
Between restraint and reinvention, stillness and movement, she exists in that rare in-between. Not fully defined, not confined. Just on the cusp of a wider sky.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

posts.caption:
If summer weddings had a couture moodboard, Alia Bhatt (@aliaabhatt’s) latest Cannes look would be on it.
Stepping out at the French Riviera in a hand-painted corset ball gown by Yash Patil (@that.antiquepiece), styled by Rhea Kapoor (@rheakapoor), Alia channels the kind of soft glamour that feels perfect for a summer cocktail soirée.
The gown’s watercolour-inspired palette, washes of mint green, lavender, blue, and pale yellow, gives it the feeling of an impressionist painting in motion, while the structured corsetry keeps the silhouette sharp and modern. Hand-painted by Basurii Chokshi (@basurii), the look makes a strong case for statement dressing that feels light enough for summer.
For brides and wedding guests navigating the season’s celebrations, consider this your cue to embrace colour, softness, and voluminous silhouettes.
#Manifest #AliaBhatt #Cannes2026 #SummerWeddingStyle #CocktailSoiree

posts.caption:
The days of giving pharmaceutical-branded swag, like like pens, mugs and even candleholders, to health care professionals are largely over. Now those items are hot on the thrifting scene.
Pharmacore — medical-branded pieces worn as fashion — has found new expression at the confluence of identity, medicine and commerce, and at a time when skepticism toward pharmaceuticals is at a high. But it's not just the vintage pieces that are finding fans. Since 2021, the Etsy shop Dr. Sign has sold more than 8,000 items featuring names and logos like Ozempic and Zoloft.
Preston Roche, a psychiatry resident and pharma swag collector, said such garments appeal for many reasons. “We’re almost mocking Big Pharma and joking about capitalism in medicine,” he said. "“Or maybe that’s just me.”
At the link in our bio, read more of the rise of pharmacore and see photos of the items vintage shoppers are seeking. Photos by Evie Hennick, Preston Roche, Gabriel Grais

posts.caption:
You want to know where men’s fashion is heading? Follow the geeks. These are the obsessives, fixated on how their pants fit, who were early to selvage denim and soft-shouldered Italian tailoring.
And where has the attention of this cohort landed now? On a vanguard of newish-to-the-West labels from Japan like A.Presse, Comoli, Auralee and T.T.
Basic though these clothes appear, their hook is that they’re opulent to the touch, elevated in their fabrication. The sudden popularity of these labels outside Japan can make it feel as if they are new. Yet each label has built a respectable business within Japan, some for more than a decade.
And they have swiftly wiggled into the broader fashion community. "While I was in Paris for the men’s fashion shows a year ago January, all anyone wanted to talk about were things with a 'Made in Japan' tag," @jacobwgallagher writes.
At the link in our bio, read more about the Japanese brands that are becoming the most talked about thing in men's fashion. Visuals by @pablodeclan, @danielledelplato, A.Presse, Auralee, ssstein

posts.caption: For all those that do not work in the apparel industry let me explain...white pants are very often worn in summer. Summer is usually warm...so pants with lining are too hot to wear. Now for those that say make them in thicker fabric - even when you double the weight youll still see pocket bags. We try different things to make the pockets less visible, we cut them in lightweight fabric so theyre more sheer, we cut them in nude lining etc.. and none of it works. The onky thing that does work is no pockets. Now we walk around with crop tops and vast areas of skin on display but heaven forbid we see legs through those pants! Good grief

posts.caption:
Caleb Hearon, a comedian known for his podcast and his stand-up, joined the world of high fashion in “The Devil Wears Prada 2.”
Alongside Meryl Streep, Stanley Tucci and Anne Hathaway, he plays an assistant to Miranda Priestly, the demanding, fictional editor of Runway magazine. Hearon’s character, nicknamed Charlie the Chair because he is not allowed to leave his desk, “is stepping into the shoes of Andy Sachs 20 years ago,” said David Frankel, who directed both films.
Hearon said it’s a role he never dreamed of booking. “It’s ‘The Devil Wears Prada’!" he said. "It is a fashion movie, and we still do not like fat people in this country. I was so pleasantly surprised at how far we’ve come.”
Hearon is circling the mainstream, and it’s obvious to those in his orbit. Regardless of his accomplishments, though, he still sees himself as the Caleb who was born and raised in rural Missouri. His Midwestern earnestness and affability draw people to him, but does his podcast-friendly personality translate into Hollywood stardom? Tap the link in our bio to read the full profile of @calebsaysthings. Photo by @voyeur1
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on May 07, 2026. May be a meme of standing, magazine and text that says 'The Comedian With The Job 'a Million Girls Would Kill For''.

posts.caption:
When “Fashion Is Art,” the dress code for the Met Gala on Monday night, was announced, it seemed like a risk. After all, fashion has been trying for decades to prove its bona fides as an equal of the other fine arts at the Metropolitan Museum of Art.
The gala has increasingly been walking the line between elegant and absurd, as guests attempt to one-up one another in the attention economy. Giving celebrities “art” as a theme could have inspired a host of old masters and Impressionism cosplay rather than any sort of consideration about what it actually means in fashion terms.
See Heidi Klum, who walked as a Grecian or Roman statue. Or Ben Platt in a Tanner Fletcher suit that evoked Georges Seurat’s “A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte.” Or Anne Hathaway, who had a Grecian figure appliquéd onto the skirt of her Michael Kors gown.
More than just flirt with costume, the Met Gala’s dress code also “acted as a reminder of the distinction between fashion and art,” wrote Vanessa Friedman, chief fashion critic for The New York Times. In her analysis, @vvfriedman examines guests’ interpretations of the dress code. Tap the link in our bio to read. Photo by @vnina
